name: Mamuthones Carnival
dates: Three times per winter: eve of January 17 (feast of Sant'Antonio Abate), Carnival Sunday, and Shrove Tuesday (February/March)
description: Annual winter carnival tradition in the village of Mamoiada, in the heart of Sardinia. The Mamuthones are costumed figures in black sheepskin cloaks wearing severe, handmade wooden masks and bearing over 60 pounds of cowbells strapped across their chests. They parade in two parallel lines, dancing rhythmically to produce a haunting clanging of bells. Alongside march the Issohadores — in bright red and white costumes with blank white masks — who carry lassos and herd the Mamuthones. On the eve of January 17, the parade weaves through the entire village at night around dozens of bonfires. The January 17 start date coincides with the feast of Sant'Antonio Abate, though the tradition predates Christianity and may have roots in the Nuragic civilization (nearly 4,000 years ago). 'It's a serious and, in some ways, sad carnival,' says longtime participant Salvatore Gungui. 'This is not like a theater for making people happy.' Tourist performances occur in daytime on Carnival Sunday and Shrove Tuesday. The village of ~2,300 depends on the festival as one of its largest tourism draws of the year.
